audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Tola, Jair, renewed apostasy, Ammonite oppression, and Gilead leadership search narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for minor-judge notices, Tola's twenty-three years, Jair's thirty sons/towns/donkeys, multi-deity apostasy list, YHWH's refusal and relenting, removal of foreign gods, Ammonite mobilization, Gilead assembly, and leadership bargain.

verses.restored: After Abimelech, Tola son of Puah son of Dodo, man of Issachar, rose to save Israel; he dwelt at Shamir in hill country of Ephraim.
He judged Israel twenty-three years; he died and was buried at Shamir.
After him Jair the Gileadite rose, and he judged Israel twenty-two years.
He had thirty sons riding on thirty donkeys, and thirty towns belonged to them; they are called Havvoth-jair to this day, which are in land of Gilead.
Jair died and was buried in Kamon.

Children of Israel again did evil in eyes of YHWH and served the Baals, Ashtaroth, gods of Aram, gods of Sidon, gods of Moab, gods of sons of Ammon, and gods of Philistines; they forsook YHWH and did not serve him.
Anger of YHWH burned against Israel, and he sold them into hand of Philistines and into hand of sons of Ammon.
They crushed and oppressed children of Israel that year; eighteen years they oppressed all children of Israel who were across Jordan in land of Amorite that is in Gilead.
Sons of Ammon crossed Jordan to fight also against Judah, Benjamin, and house of Ephraim, and Israel was greatly distressed.
Children of Israel cried out to YHWH, saying, We have sinned/misaligned against you, because we have forsaken our Divine and served the Baals.
YHWH said to children of Israel, Did I not save you from Egypt, Amorite, sons of Ammon, Philistines,
Sidonians, Amalek, and Maon oppressed you, and you cried out to me, and I saved you from their hand?
But you have forsaken me and served other gods; therefore I will not continue to save you.
Go and cry out to gods whom you chose; let them save you in time of your distress.
Children of Israel said to YHWH, We have sinned/misaligned. You do to us according to all good in your eyes; only rescue us, please, this day.
They removed foreign gods from their midst and served YHWH, and his soul/life was shortened/impatient/grieved because of misery of Israel.
Sons of Ammon were called together and camped in Gilead; children of Israel gathered and camped at Mizpah.
People, leaders of Gilead, said each to his neighbor, Who is the man who will begin to fight against sons of Ammon? He shall be head over all inhabitants of Gilead.

translation_notes: Tola and Jair are brief 'minor judge' notices, preserving periods of leadership without detailed war narratives.
Jair's thirty sons, thirty donkeys, and thirty towns signal status, local power, and possible proto-aristocratic structure, especially after Abimelech's failed kingship.

The long list of gods in verse 6 expands apostasy beyond Baal/Ashtaroth and portrays Israel as serving powers from every surrounding region. This is covenant polemic, not permission to demean modern religions.
YHWH's refusal in verses 13-14 dramatizes the exhaustion of rescue cycles, but the chapter does not end with abandonment.
Verse 16 is emotionally striking: YHWH's nephesh is shortened/grieved by Israel's misery. Divine response is moved by suffering even after rebuke.
The people remove foreign gods and serve YHWH before any named deliverer appears, making repentance concrete but not transactional magic.
The final leadership question offers headship to whoever starts the fight, setting up Jephthah and exposing how crisis can create dangerous bargains for power.
safety_note: Do not use Judges 10 to mock other religions, claim Divine contempt for sufferers, or teach that rescue must be earned through perfect repentance. Center the grief of oppression, concrete turning, Divine anguish over misery, and caution about leadership deals made under military pressure.
